data.title: Plunging oil prices force U.S. oil industry to consider cutting production, jobs
data.description:
Despite President Trump's exhortations to "drill, baby, drill," U.S. shale oil producers are facing their most serious crisis since the pandemic, as the sudden oil price selloff triggered in part by President Trump's trade war has pushed parts of the sectorВ to the brink of failure, industry executives warned this week.

Oil markets have been hit with a potential "double whammy" of declining demand from a tariff-induced economic slowdown combined with new supplies, with OPEC+ announcing last week it planned to raise production faster than expected in the coming months.

Analysts say Trump's decision to leave tariffs on China, the world's biggest oil importer, will continue to loom over global crude demand prospects.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration this week sharply cut its estimate of U.S. crude prices to $63.88/bbl for 2025 from a prior forecast of $70.68/bb, and lowered its forecast for global oil consumption growth for this year by 400K bbl/day to 900K bbl/day.

Tariffs also will make it more expensive to buy steel and equipment, which could further discourage drilling unless oil prices rise substantially.

Benchmark U.S. crude has fallen nearly 14% so far this month, leaving the price below the level many producers in Texas say they need to break even and even causing concern that the industry could be forced to idle rigs.

Rystad Energy said this week that many U.S. shale producers faced breakeven costs of ~$62/bbl when debt servicing and dividend payments were included, and "in reality, even a company operating on $40 breakeven acreage would be inclined to slow down activity when prices fall below $65/bbl, as their level of dividend coverage would be at risk," Rystad said.

Energy prices remain well below levels producers say they need before they substantially increase activity and will not get there any time soon, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City's latest quarterly survey, which said the energy firms surveyed need to see oil at $85/bbl and natural gas at $5.10/MMBtu before they significantly ramp up drilling.

"If oil does go into lower $60s or upper $50s, public independents that are already capital disciplined are going to have to cut their budget and cut rigs," said Bryan Sheffield, founder of energy investors Formentera Partners and former Parsely Energy CEO.

"If prices get sub-$60 and stay there, we'll see a definite drop in the rig count," Marauder Capital managing partner Roe Patterson said. "They've definitely opened the door for the OPEC countries to gain market share here, and it's an inadvertent, self-inflicted wound."

While it may cost under $40/bbl to drill in the best parts of the Permian Basin, new well drilling in North Dakota would require oil prices at ~$57/bbl, according to Wood Mackenzie, meaning operations in those basins would be more at risk at current price levels.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:XLE), (NYSEARCA:XOP), (AMLP), (VDE), (OIH), (IXC), (CRAK)

data.title: KKR nearing deal to acquire OSTTRA for around $3B: report
data.description:
KKR (NYSE:KKR) is close to a deal to acquire post-trade services provider OSTTRA for around $3B, Bloomberg reported Saturday, citing people familiar with the matter.В 

According to the report, KKRвЂ™s talks for the joint venture of CME Group (CME) and S&P Global (SPGI) are in the advanced stages after it beat out other possible buyers, the people said.В 

A transaction could be announced as soon as next week, the people were quoted as saying.В 

GTCR, Advent International, CVC Capital Partners (OTC:CVCCF) and other private-equity firms had been after OSTTR, Bloomberg said.В 

Negotiations are continuing and a deal may be delayed or fall through, the people reportedly said.В 

KKR, OSTTRA, CME Group, S&P Global, GTCR, Advent International, and CVC Capital Partner did not immediately respond to requests for comment by Seeking Alpha

data.title: Trump exempts smartphones, computers, chips from reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
Update 4:25 PM ET: Adds details, Rewrites throughoutВ 

President Donald Trump's administration on Friday exempted smartphones, computers and other electronics mostly imported from China from reciprocal tariffs, providing major relief for American tech players like Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA)В and Dell (NYSE:DELL).В 

The exclusions, published by the U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency, follow Trump's moves to impose 145% tariffs on products from China and 10% baseline tariffs on almost every other nation.В 

The 20 categories listed in the notice are exempt from 125% tariffs on Chinese imports and 10% baseline tariffs on other countries. Goods from China will still face a 20% tariff, a levy imposed to force Beijing to crack down on fentanyl.В 

The exemptions are applicable to popular electronics items such asВ smartphones, laptops, computers, computer processors, memory chips, flat panel TV displays, solar cells, hard drives.В 

The guidance should provide some respite to smartphone manufacturers like Apple (AAPL) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), AI major Nvidia (NVDA), server makers like Super Micro (NASDAQ:SMCI) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE), computer makers like Dell (DELL) and HP (NYSE:HPQ), and chip majorВ Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM).В 

Tech bull Dan Ives, who slashed price targetsВ for Magnificent 7 names like Apple (AAPL) and Tesla (TSLA) over Trump's tariffs, called the exclusions "the best news possible for tech investors."В 

"Big Tech is back off the cliff with these exemptions and this changes the entire situation for tech stocks with this black swan event for the industry removed," said Ives. "The U.S. tech industry has a loud voice and despite initial strong pushback against exemptions within the White House the reality of the situation was finally recognized in the Beltway."

A Win for Apple

The exemptions coverВ almost $390 billion in U.S. imports, including more than $101 billion from China, Bloomberg reported, citing data compiled byВ Gerard DiPippo, associate director of the Rand China Research Center.

The biggest exempt category for Chinese goods is smartphones, which accounted for nearly $42 billion in U.S. imports in 2024.В 

Although Apple (AAPL) has shifted some of its production to countries like India and Vietnam, it still assembles most of its devices in China.

It'sВ estimated that at least 50% of iPhone production comes from China, along with more than 50% of Macs and 75% to 80% of iPads.

Apple (AAPL) reportedly airlifted 600 tons of iPhones, or as many as 1.5 million, from India to the U.S., in an effort to beat the previously announced tariffs.

data.title: Trump tariffs stall bitcoin's rise now, but fuel long-term growth
data.description:
President Donald Trump's tariff moves have been about reasserting U.S. leverage in global trade, but the policy execution has been wildly aggressive and unpredictable, jolting the crypto market and virtually all other asset classes amid fears of trade wars and recession.В 

Interestingly, bitcoin (BTC-USD) managed to hold steady last week above $80K, when the initial tariff announcement on April 2 roiled ex-crypto markets. At the time, the token's stability signaled that it may be living up to expectations of being a safe haven and independent of the mainstream financial system. But it didn't take long for bitcoin to catch up with the global rout,В dropping to below $75K just early this week.В 

As the week powered on, Trump abruptly announced a tariff pause while significantly raising the levy on Chinese imports, a move that triggered a massive rebound in asset prices. Since then, bitcoin has been hovering above the $80K mark as uncertainty prevails, last changing hands at $82.3K as of Friday afternoon.

All told, bitcoin (BTC-USD) was downВ less than 3% since "Liberation Day," faring better than the S&P 500's (SP500) 7.1% drop, as well as crude oil's 17% tumble and long-dated Treasury bonds' (TLT) 5.5% decline. Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) actually advanced 1.7%, while the U.S. dollar (DXY) was also off byВ about 3%.

"BitcoinвЂ™s price volatility has increased by much less than equity volatility, and a range of indicators suggest that speculative trader positioning in crypto is relatively low," crypto asset manager Grayscale said in a research report. "If macro risks ease over the coming weeks, crypto valuations should be poised to rebound, in our view.вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЌвЂ‹вЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂЊвЂЊвЂЌвЂЊвЂ‹вЂ‹вЂЌвЂ‹вЂЌвЂЊвЂЊ"

What happens next over the near term will be dependent on trade discussions between Trump and other nations, Author Zach Pandl wrote. "Although negotiations could result in lower tariffs, setbacks in the talks could also result in more retaliation."

Even with Trump's 90-day tariff pause, the average effective U.S. tariff rate remains the highest in a century. Grayscale believes trade tensions -- namely with China -- and higher tariffs "should be considered positive for bitcoin adoption over the medium term." He cited stagflation risks induced by tariffs (that is, the simultaneous advance in inflation and fall in economic growth/employment) as a negative for traditional risk assets like equities, but a positive for scarce commodities such as gold (XAUUSD:USD).

Grayscale is in the camp that bitcoin (BTC-USD) is akin to digital hold, or a modern store of value in times of turmoil.

"Trade tensions may put pressure on reserve demand for the U.S. Dollar, opening space for competing assets, including other fiat currencies, gold, and bitcoin," the report said.

Conversely, in a Feb. 6 note, CoinShares analyst James Butterfill reckoned that tariffs would be negative for bitcoin (BTC-USD) over the short term, on the view that slower economic growth would temporarily reduce demand for risk assets.

"Unlike gold, Bitcoin has a growth component, meaning it reacts to economic trends and liquidity cycles," the note said.

data.title: Alphabet, Nvidia invest in AI startup Safe Superintelligence: report
data.description:
Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) have invested in Safe Superintellgence, an AI startup co-founded by ex-OpenAI chief scientist Ilya Sutskever that launched last month, Reuters reported late Friday, citing a person familiar with the matter.В 

According to the report, the size of the investments could not be determined.В 

People reportedly said SSI was valued recently at $32B in a funding round headed by Greenoaks.В В 

Safe Superintelligence, Alphabet, and Nvidia did not immediately respond to requests for comment by Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Executive reshuffles: CVS, NNDM, and DBX
data.description:
Market watchers, throughout the week, observed several Wall Street executives stepping up for new roles as part of leadership reshuffling.

Meanwhile, some companies such as CVS Health, Nano Dimension and Healthcare Realty added new positions to steer growth prospects.

Here are some of the recent management changes that took place during the week:

CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) finance chief Thomas Cowhey will step down amid the company's ongoing turnaround efforts led by CEO David Joyn. Brian NewmanВ has been named executive vice president and CFO designate, effective April 21.

Nano Dimension (NASDAQ:NNDM) has named Ofir Baharav as its new CEO, effective immediately. In addition, Zivi Nedivi, president, and Tomer Pinchas, CFO and COO, are departing the company.

Dropbox (NASDAQ:DBX) has said that its Chief Customer Officer Eric Cox plans to step down.

Healthcare Realty (HR) has appointedВ Peter ScottВ as its CEO and president.В Connie Moore, who has served as interim CEO and president since November 2024, will transition out of that role and continue to serve on the board.

Golden Ocean (GOGL) has announced theВ appointmentВ of Peder Simonsen as the CEO of Golden Ocean Management AS. Simonsen, who currently holds the roles of Interim CEO and CFO of Golden Ocean Management AS, will now take on the CEO position permanently.

BioCryst Pharmaceuticals (BCRX) said on Thursday that it hasВ appointedВ Jon P. Stonehouse, the companyвЂ™s CEO, to serve as interim CFO and interim Principal Accounting Officer, effective as of the effective date.

CNH (CNH) has named Jim Nickolas as its next CFO, effective May 6, replacing Oddone Incisa.

Allison Transmission (ALSN) has announced the appointment of Scott Mell as its new CFO and treasurer, effective April 14, 2025.

data.title: Key deals this week: Capri, WNS Holdings, Fintech Payoneer, Altus Power and more
data.description:
Here's a list of key deals reported across sectors this week:

Capri Holdings (NYSE:CPRI) rose 8.3% amid reports that Prada (OTCPK:PRDSF) is close to acquiring the Versace brand for about $1.38 billion.

WNS Holdings (NYSE:WNS) rose 1.8% after a report that Capgemini SE (OTCPK:CAPMF) is in advanced discussions to purchase the outsourcing firm.

Fintech Payoneer Global (NASDAQ:PAYO) announced on Wednesday the close of the acquisition of a licensed China-based payment service provider, Easylink Payment.

Wellgistics Health (NASDAQ:WGRX) has entered into a definitive agreement to acquire Peek Healthcare to expand its digital healthcare ecosystem.

PainReform (NASDAQ:PRFX) announced on Thursday the acquisition of DeepSolar, a provider of AI-driven software solutions designed to optimize solar energy usage in residential and utility-scale solar power plants.

Altus Power (NYSE:AMPS) said Wednesday that shareholders voted to approve the previously announced agreement for the company's acquisition by TPG through its TPG Rise Climate Transition Infrastructure strategy.

Ripple Labs, the company behind the XRP (XRP-USD) cryptocurrency, agreed to buy multi-asset prime brokerage firm Hidden Road for $1.25B, a deal that's expected to bolster Ripple's efforts on the stablecoin front, it said on Tuesday.

Germany's Infineon Technologies (OTCQX:IFNNY) agreed to purchase Marvell Technology's automotive ethernet business for about $2.5 billion in cash. MarvellВ rose 3%В in after-hours trading.

Ki and Public Storage (NYSE:PSA) have submitted a non-binding indicative offer to acquire Abacus Storage King (ASK) for A$1.47 per stapled security. The acquisition targets all outstanding securities not already held by Ki Group, ASK's major security holder.

В

data.title: Trending stocks as Wall Street recovers from recent volatility
data.description:
After stocks tanked in the aftermath of US President Donald TrumpвЂ™s tariff shocks last week, Wall Street seemed to have steadied itself and shook off some of the bearishness this week.

The S&P 500 jumped 7.8% over the week, similar to the Dow Jones Industrial Average. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite was up 9.2%.

Investors reacted positively to Trump issuing a 90-day tariff pause and Boston Fed President Susan Collins assuring that the Fed would вЂњabsolutely be preparedвЂќ to help stabilize financial markets if required.

However, caution still reigned in the market, with uncertainties around TrumpвЂ™s rollercoaster trade policies and countriesвЂ™ reactions to them.

The week also saw the first quarter earnings season start with major U.S. banks releasing their latest quarterly reports.

Amid another wild week for Wall Street, these were some of the trending stocks:

US Steel (X) shares fell on Wednesday after President Donald Trump said he didnвЂ™t want a Japanese company to buy the Pittsburgh-based steelmaker. Earlier, on Monday, Trump ordered a national security review of its planned sale to Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY). The President also added later on Thursday that he likes Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY) as an investor and doesn't want the U.S. steelmaker sold to a Japanese company.

CVS (CVS), Humana (HUM), UnitedHealth (UNH) and other managed-care stocks rose on Tuesday after the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services finalized a 5.06% average increase in payments to Medicare Advantage plans from 2025 to 2026, a sharp increase from the 2.23% the agency proposed as part of its Advance Notice in January.

Marvell Technology (MRVL) was in news after the chipmaker agreed to sell its automotive ethernet business for $2.5B in cash to Infineon Technologies.

Coal stocks gained on Tuesday as President Donald Trump signed executive orders intended to expand the production and use of coal in the U.S. Peabody Energy (BTU) rose 9.2%, Ramaco Resources (METC) +13.9%, Core Natural Resources (CNR) +6.6%, Warrior Met Coal (HCC) +6.3%, Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP) +4.8%, Alpha Metallurgical Resources (AMR) +2.4%, SunCoke Energy (SXC) +1.2%.

Easterly Government Properties (DEA) erased over 18% after the company announced a 32% cut to its quarterly dividend, lowering it to $0.18 per share from $0.265. The board also approved a 1-for-2.5 reverse stock split.

Trump Media (DJT) rose as much as 22.4% on Wednesday, notching its best gains since January this year. In a post on his social media platform Truth Social, Donald Trump said, "THIS IS A GREAT TIME TO BUY!!! DJT." The post came hours before the announcement of the 90-day pause on the U.S. reciprocal tariffs unveiled by him earlier this month.

CarMax (KMX) lost shares fall as much as 17% on Thursday after the used-car retailerвЂ™s earnings per share and comparable store sales for the fourth quarter missed market expectations. The company also removed timeframes associated with its long-term goals, citing вЂњthe potential impact of broader macro factors.вЂќ

Shares in companies working on biotech AI models gained after the FDA said on Thursday it plans to phase out animal testing requirements for monoclonal antibodies and other drugs. Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX), Absci (ABSI), Certara (CERT), Schrodinger (SDGR) and Nuvation Bio (NUVB) were some of the notable gainers.

Shares of major European drugmakers, including AstraZeneca (AZN), GSK (GSK), Novartis (NVS), Sanofi (SNY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO), fell Wednesday morning after U.S. President Donald Trump reaffirmed plans to impose a "major" tariff on all pharmaceutical imports.

data.title: Real estate stocks down marginally amid the broader bond selloff, rising yields
data.description:
Real estate stocks lost ground in a week that saw the benchmark S&P 500 index advance more than 5% amid the broader bond selloffВ and rising long-term Treasury yields.

The United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) rose 48 basis points during the course of the week to 4.49%, while the United States 20-Year Bond Yield (US20Y) was up 50 basis points to 4.94%. The United States 30-Year Bond Yield (US30Y) increased 45 basis points to 4.87%.

Traditionally, yields fall as investors seek safety by buying bonds during equity market stress. However, that traditional safe-haven role has been called into question amid this week's broader bond selloff.

While the equity market swings have garnered most of the attention this week with markets notching extreme highs and lows, Barclays argued that swings in the bond market have been the "most worrisome part of price action" that took place so far this week.

The investment institution went on to add that "until Treasuries stabilize and start to behave normally, risk assets will struggle, in our view".

Barclays analysts say the recent volatility in the bond market remains somewhat of a mystery. One possible explanation the institution pointed to is investors who may have entered swap spread widening positions, expecting relief on the Supplementary Leverage Ratio, only to face pressure as those bets moved against them.

Another theory involves the unwinding of the "basis trade". There's also ongoing speculation that foreign investorsвЂ”particularly from AsiaвЂ”may be behind the selling activity.

Total returns across REITs have recently been exhibiting an inverse relationship with the US10Y changes.

The FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs index (INDEXFTSE: FNER) dippedВ 2.82%В on a weekly basis to 718.38. Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (NASDAQ:REIT:IND) was downВ 0.58%В to 2,468.87.

The S&P 500 Real Estate Index Sector (SP500-60) retreatedВ 0.16%В from last week to 244.25, while the accompanying The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) fellВ 0.10%В to 38.92.

XLRE gainedВ 5.74%В on Wednesday to close at 39.21 when the Wall Street notched its best day since the financial crisis of 2008. The day saw the Trump administration announce a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs against most countries.

But the gains could not offset the losses logged in most trading sessions of the week.

The ETF wasВ -2.41%В to 38.02 on Monday, logging outflows of $42.86M. XLRE dippedВ 2.47%В to 37.08 on Tuesday, having seen outflows of $45.63M. The fund saw outflows of $21.57M on Thursday, recording losses ofВ 2.12%В to 38.38 after Wednesday's gain.

XLRE gets aВ SellВ rating from Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system and aВ BuyВ rating from SA analysts.

Earnings Season

Amidst the tariff frenzy, a primarily greenВ earnings scorecard brought much needed cheer to the markets. Out of the ten S&P 500 companies that reported earnings this week, eight of them beat EPS expectations, while all of them topped revenue estimates.

For next week, the S&P 500 real estate company reporting Q1 earnings is Prologis (PLD). TheВ consensus FFO estimate is $1.38 (+7.87% Y/Y) and the consensus revenue estimate is $1.99B (+9.01% Y/Y).

Other major real estate names reporting next week are Manhattan Bridge Capital (LOAN), First Industrial Realty Trust (FR), SL Green Realty (SLG), and Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS).

Real estate as an S&P 500 sector has seen upward revisions to revenue estimates and positive revenue surprises since the end of the quarter. The sector has seen an increase to its blended Q1 revenue growth rate to 4.1% from 3.7%, an April 11 report by FactSetВ showed.

Real estate ranks second among the four S&P 500 sectors to record an increase in its revenue growth rate, or a decrease in revenue decline, since the end of the quarter, the report noted.

However, the sector takes the lead among the six S&P 500 sectors expected to report a year-over-year decline in its blended Q1 net profit margin. The metric is expected to drop to 34.5% from 36.2% for the quarter, according to FactSet.

Winners & Losers

Here are the top S&P 500 real estate losers of the week:

  • Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE)В -5.36%
  • Crown Castle (CCI)В -4.27%
  • American Tower (AMT)В -3.16%
  • Extra Space Storage (EXR)В -3.14%
  • Essex Property Trust (ESS)В -2.79%

Here are the top S&P 500 real estate gainers of the week:

  • Iron Mountain (IRM)В +8.10%
  • CoStar Group (CSGP)В +6.57%
  • Digital Realty Trust (DLR)В +5.52%
  • Host Hotels & Resorts (HST)В +4.34%
  • Kimco Realty (KIM)В +4.19%

data.title: White House preparing order to allow stockpiling metals from Pacific bottom: report
data.description:
The White House is preparing an executive order that will allow for stockpiling of metals found on the seabed of the Pacific Ocean, the Financial Times reported Saturday, citing people familiar with the matter.В 

According to the report, the order would be a push to challenge the dominance by China of battery minerals and rare earth supply chains, the people reportedly said.В 

The U.S. has been seeking to become more self-sufficient in accessing the minerals and announced moves to boost domestic output.В 

The stockpile under consideration is part of a wider drive to fast-track applications for deep-sea mining and to establish onshore processing of nodules, the people familiar with the plans were quoted as saying.В 

A stockpile would вЂњcreate large quantities ready and available on US territory to be used in the futureвЂќ if there is a dispute with China that would hinder metals and rare earths imports, another person familiar with the matter was quoted as saying.В 

The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment by Seeking Alpha.

В
